title: Listening - Part 4: Captaining an Island Ferry
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with Rosa Martin, the captain of an island ferry. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: My guest is Rosa Martin, captain of a passenger ferry that connects two island communities with the mainland. Rosa, did you grow up expecting to work at sea? || Rosa: My father repaired boats, but I actually planned to become a teacher. While studying, I travelled on the ferry every week to visit home. [24]I became curious about how the crew handled changing sea conditions while still making the journey feel calm and ordinary for passengers[/24]. I took a summer job on deck, postponed teacher training and eventually qualified as a captain. || Presenter: What was hardest during your training? || Rosa: Learning navigation and engines was demanding, but I enjoyed the technical side. What took longer was accepting the wider responsibility. [25]As captain, every decision about sailing or delaying a journey affects the safety of many passengers, not simply my own progress[/25]. That responsibility becomes normal, but it never becomes unimportant. || Presenter: Does travelling the same route become boring? || Rosa: Not really. The route on a map is identical, but the sea is not. [26]Tides, wind and visibility mean that even a familiar crossing requires fresh attention each time we leave harbour[/26]. Routine is useful because the crew works efficiently, but we never assume one journey will be exactly like the last. Passengers may not notice those small adjustments, which is probably a good thing. || Presenter: You carry many regular passengers. Do you get to know them? || Rosa: Yes, and that is one of the pleasures of the job. [27]Regular travellers depend on us for work, school or medical appointments, so providing a reliable connection feels genuinely important[/27]. We know many faces, although on busy days there isn’t time for long conversations. || Presenter: What do tourists find difficult? || Rosa: Some people on holiday think a timetable is a promise whatever the conditions. [28]When a sailing is delayed, I try to explain that waiting in harbour can be the safest decision, even if the weather looks acceptable from land[/28]. Most people understand once they know the reason. || Presenter: Could ferries become more environmentally friendly? || Rosa: Absolutely, and new electric vessels are exciting. But island routes have practical limits. [29]Before our ferry can use battery power successfully, the small harbours need reliable charging facilities and enough time between journeys[/29]. The vessel alone is not the whole solution. || Presenter: What advice would you give someone attracted to this career? || Rosa: Study the technical qualifications, naturally, but don’t overlook people. [30]A captain must communicate clearly with passengers and crew, especially when plans change or somebody is anxious[/30]. Good seamanship includes making others feel informed and safe.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: What made Rosa consider a career on ferries?

options.A: She wanted to continue her father's boat business.
options.B: She admired the crew during her journeys home.
options.C: She disliked her course in teacher training.

questions.explanation: As a regular passenger, Rosa became interested in how crews managed changing sea conditions calmly.

questions.question: What did Rosa find most challenging about becoming a captain?

options.A: Being responsible for the safety of many other people.
options.C: Navigating without help from her crew.

questions.explanation: She found the responsibility for passengers' safety harder to adjust to than the technical skills.

questions.question: Why does Rosa say the route does not become boring?

options.A: The passengers are different on every crossing.
options.B: The crew regularly uses different harbours.
options.C: Conditions make every crossing require attention.

questions.explanation: Tides, wind and visibility make each journey different despite following the same route.

questions.question: What does Rosa like about carrying regular passengers?

options.A: Her service plays an important role in their daily lives.
options.B: They are more patient than tourists during delays.
options.C: She has time to talk to them during journeys.

questions.explanation: Regular passengers rely on the service for essential daily activities.

questions.question: What does Rosa explain to tourists?

options.A: The weather changes more quickly on islands.
options.B: A delay can be necessary for safety.
options.C: Ferry timetables change in the summer.

questions.explanation: She explains that staying in harbour may be the safe decision even when land weather seems fine.

questions.question: What is needed before her route can use an electric ferry effectively?
questions.explanation: Rosa says ports need reliable charging facilities and suitable time between sailings.

questions.question: What ability does Rosa consider essential for a captain?
questions.explanation: She stresses clear communication with passengers and crew, especially in altered plans.
